title: Income Tax Fundamentals
description: Calculate taxable income through the tax formula and understand deductions, filing status, and tax rates.
topics: Calculate taxable income through the tax formula
Compare filing status options and implications
Apply marginal vs. effective tax rate concepts

sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: Understanding the structure of income tax is essential for effective tax planning. This lesson covers the fundamental tax formula, deductions, filing status, and marginal versus effective tax rates.

sections.title: Why AGI Matters
sections.content: Many tax benefits phase out based on AGI: Child tax credit, IRA deduction limits, Education credits, and Medicare premium surcharges (IRMAA). Reducing AGI through above-the-line deductions can unlock additional tax benefits.

sections.title: Standard vs. Itemized Decision
sections.content: Itemize if: Total itemized deductions > Standard deduction. Otherwise, take the standard deduction for simplicity and maximum benefit.

sections.title: Five Filing Statuses
rows: Spouse died within 2 years + dependent child

sections.title: Head of Household Requirements
items: Unmarried (or considered unmarried)
Pay more than half household costs
Qualifying person lived with you more than half year

sections.title: Marginal vs. Effective Tax Rate
sections.content: Marginal Rate: The rate on the next dollar of income. Effective Rate: The average rate on all income, calculated as Total Tax ÷ Taxable Income.

sections.title: Example: Single Filer with $100,000 Taxable Income
sections.content: 10% on first $11,600 = $1,160 | 12% on next $35,550 = $4,266 | 22% on next $53,375 = $11,743 | 24% on remaining $11,475 = $2,754. Total Tax: $19,923. Marginal rate: 24%. Effective rate: 19.9%.

sections.title: Why Credits Are More Valuable
sections.content: $1,000 deduction in 22% bracket = $220 tax savings. $1,000 credit = $1,000 tax savings. Credits are more valuable!

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Tax formula: Gross income → AGI → taxable income → tax
Above-the-line deductions reduce AGI; below-the-line reduce taxable income
Standard vs. itemized: Use whichever is greater
Marginal rate: Rate on next dollar; Effective rate: Average rate
Credits > Deductions: $1 credit = $1 tax reduction

knowledgeCheck.question: John and Mary are married filing jointly. John earned a salary of $80,000 and Mary earned a salary of $60,000. They contributed $5,000 to a traditional IRA (John $3,000, Mary $2,000). They are taking the standard deduction. Assuming the standard deduction for married filing jointly is $27,700 and they are both under age 50, what is their taxable income?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Their gross income is $80,000 + $60,000 = $140,000. Their above-the-line deduction (IRA contribution) is $5,000. Their adjusted gross income (AGI) is $140,000 - $5,000 = $135,000. Since they are taking the standard deduction, their taxable income is $135,000 - $27,700 = $107,300.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember to subtract above-the-line deductions from gross income to get AGI, then subtract below-the-line deductions to get taxable income. Standard deductions are below the line.

reveal.question: Name three common above-the-line deductions (deductions for adjusted gross income).
reveal.answer: Common above-the-line deductions include: 1) Traditional IRA contributions (subject to limitations), 2) Student loan interest payments (subject to limitations), and 3) Health savings account (HSA) contributions. Other possibilities: Educator expenses, self-employment tax, alimony paid (for divorce decrees executed before 2019).

rows.label: Definition
values: The tax rate applied to the last dollar of income.
The actual percentage of total income paid in taxes.

rows.label: Calculation
values: Determined by the tax bracket in which taxable income falls.
Total tax liability divided by total income (AGI or gross income, depending on context).

rows.label: Use
values: Useful for determining the tax impact of additional income or deductions.
Provides a more accurate picture of the overall tax burden.

title: Types of Income and Special Rules
description: Distinguish ordinary income from capital gains and understand passive activity loss limitations.
topics: Distinguish ordinary income from capital gains
Apply qualified dividend and capital gains rates
Identify tax treatment of various income types

sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: Different types of income receive different tax treatment. Understanding these distinctions is crucial for tax planning.

sections.title: Net Investment Income Tax (NIIT)
sections.content: Additional 3.8% on investment income if MAGI exceeds $250,000 (MFJ) or $200,000 (Single). Maximum LTCG rate: 23.8% (20% + 3.8%).

sections.title: Capital Loss Netting Process
items: Net short-term gains and losses
Net long-term gains and losses
Net the two results together

sections.title: Annual Capital Loss Deduction Limit
sections.content: Capital losses can offset unlimited capital gains, plus $3,000 of ordinary income per year. Excess carries forward indefinitely.

sections.title: Capital Loss Netting Example
sections.content: Short-term gain: $5,000 | Short-term loss: ($8,000) | Long-term gain: $10,000 | Long-term loss: ($2,000). Net ST: -$3,000 | Net LT: +$8,000. Overall: +$5,000 net long-term gain.

sections.title: Qualified Dividend Requirements
rows: Not money market funds or certain preferred

sections.title: Interest Income Treatment
rows: Exempt from federal, may be exempt from state

sections.title: Passive Loss Limitations
rows: Passive losses offset passive income only
Cannot offset wages or portfolio income
Until future passive income or disposition
Suspended losses allowed when you sell

sections.title: Real Estate Professional Exception
sections.content: Can treat rental income as non-passive if: More than 750 hours in real estate activities, more than half of working time in real estate, and material participation in each rental activity.

sections.title: Rental Loss Exception Requirements
items: Active participation (approve tenants, expenses)
Own at least 10% of property
Not passive investor

sections.title: At-Risk Rules
sections.content: Can only deduct losses to the extent of: Cash invested, Property basis, and Borrowed amounts for which you're personally liable. Cannot deduct losses from non-recourse loans (unless qualified real estate).

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Ordinary income: 10-37%; includes wages, interest, ST gains
LTCG/qualified dividends: 0%, 15%, 20% (+ 3.8% NIIT if applicable)
Capital losses: Offset gains first, then $3,000 ordinary income
Passive losses: Only offset passive income (suspended otherwise)
$25,000 rental exception: Phases out between $100K-$150K MAGI

knowledgeCheck.question: John and Mary are married filing jointly and have taxable income of $100,000. They received $5,000 in qualified dividends and realized a $10,000 long-term capital gain. What is their total tax liability on the qualified dividends and long-term capital gain, assuming no other factors apply?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The 0% rate applies to taxpayers in the 10% and 12% tax brackets. For 2024, the 12% tax bracket ends at $89,450 for married filing jointly. Since their taxable income is $100,000, a portion of their income is taxed at higher rates. However, the qualified dividends and long-term capital gains are taxed at the preferential rates. Because their taxable income is $100,000, the 0% bracket for capital gains and dividends is fully utilized. Therefore, the entire $15,000 is taxed at 0%.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider the tax brackets for qualified dividends and long-term capital gains.

reveal.question: What is the general rule regarding the deductibility of passive activity losses?
reveal.answer: Passive activity losses can only be deducted to the extent of passive activity income. Undeducted losses are carried forward indefinitely and can be deducted when the passive activity is disposed of in a fully taxable transaction.

rows.label: Definition
values: Income derived from wages, salaries, tips, interest, rents, and other sources not considered capital gains.
Income derived from the sale of capital assets (e.g., stocks, bonds, real estate).

rows.label: Tax Rate
values: Taxed at ordinary income tax rates, which vary depending on the taxpayer's income bracket.
Taxed at preferential rates (0%, 15%, or 20%) depending on the holding period and the taxpayer's income bracket; short-term capital gains are taxed as ordinary income.

rows.label: Examples
values: Wages, Salaries, Interest Income, Rental Income
Profit from the sale of stock held for more than one year, Profit from the sale of real estate held for more than one year

title: Capital Gains Tax Planning
description: Apply capital gains taxation to investment decisions and implement tax-efficient gain/loss harvesting.
topics: Apply capital gains taxation to investment decisions
Calculate cost basis using different methods
Evaluate wash sale and related party rules

sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: Strategic management of capital gains and losses can significantly reduce lifetime tax burden.

sections.title: Net Investment Income Tax
sections.content: +3.8% on investment income if MAGI exceeds $250,000 (MFJ) or $200,000 (Single).

sections.title: Cost Basis Formula
sections.content: Gain/Loss = Amount Realized - Adjusted Basis

sections.title: Determining Basis
rows: Donor's basis (dual basis rule for losses)
Fair market value at date of death

sections.title: Gift Basis Rules
rows: FMV < Donor's basis, sold at gain
FMV < Donor's basis, sold at loss

sections.title: Inherited Property: Step-Up in Basis
sections.content: Inherited property receives basis equal to FMV at date of death. This is a powerful tax benefit that eliminates unrealized gains.

sections.title: Step-Up Example
sections.content: Decedent's basis: $50,000 | FMV at death: $200,000 | Heir's basis: $200,000 | If sold for $210,000: $10,000 gain (not $160,000).

sections.title: Step-Up Planning Implications
items: Don't sell appreciated assets before death if leaving to heirs
Consider gifting assets with LOSS (no step-up for losses)
Low-basis assets ideal to hold until death

sections.title: Wash Sale Rule
sections.content: Cannot deduct loss if you purchase substantially identical security within 30 days before or after the sale.

sections.title: What Triggers Wash Sale
rows: Buy similar but not identical ETF

sections.title: Wash Sale Consequence
sections.content: Loss is disallowed (not permanently lost). Disallowed loss is added to new shares' basis. Holding period of old shares tacks on.

sections.title: Tax-Gain Harvesting Strategy
sections.content: Realize gains when in low or 0% bracket. Consider during: Early retirement (low income year), Year with large losses to offset, Rebalancing needed, Before expected rate increase.

sections.title: Tax-Gain Harvesting Example
sections.content: Retired couple with $80,000 taxable income (MFJ): 0% LTCG threshold is $94,050. Can realize $14,050 in LTCG at 0% rate.

sections.title: Tax-Loss Harvesting Strategy
sections.content: Realize losses to: Offset capital gains, Offset $3,000 ordinary income, Carry forward excess.

sections.title: Tax-Loss Harvesting Best Practices
items: Avoid wash sales: Keep 31+ day gap
Use similar replacement: Stay invested in market
Harvest throughout year: Don't wait until December
Consider ST vs LT: Match character when possible
Document everything: Track for carryforward

sections.title: Netting Order
items: Short-term gains vs. short-term losses
Long-term gains vs. long-term losses (by rate bracket)
Net short-term vs. net long-term

sections.title: Partial Exclusion
sections.content: If requirements not fully met due to work, health, or unforeseen circumstances, a pro-rata portion of exclusion may apply.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Hold >1 year for preferential LTCG rates (0%, 15%, 20%)
Inherited property: Step-up to FMV at death (huge benefit)
Wash sale: 30-day window, substantially identical; loss added to basis
Tax-gain harvest in 0% bracket years; tax-loss harvest to offset gains
Home sale: $250K/$500K exclusion if owned and lived 2 of 5 years

knowledgeCheck.question: An investor purchased 100 shares of XYZ stock at $50 per share in January, another 100 shares at $60 per share in March, and sold 100 shares in December at $70 per share. If the investor uses the FIFO (First-In, First-Out) method to determine cost basis, what is their capital gain?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: With FIFO, the first shares purchased are considered the first sold. Therefore, the 100 shares purchased in January at $50 are used to calculate the gain. The capital gain is (Selling Price - Cost Basis) = ($70 * 100) - ($50 * 100) = $2,000.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember FIFO means the first shares you bought are considered the first shares you sold.

reveal.question: Explain the Wash Sale Rule and its purpose.
reveal.answer: The Wash Sale Rule prevents investors from claiming a loss on the sale of stock or securities if they purchase substantially identical securities within 30 days before or after the sale. The disallowed loss is added to the basis of the newly purchased stock. Its purpose is to prevent taxpayers from artificially generating tax losses without significantly altering their investment position.

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following is TRUE regarding capital losses incurred from selling an asset to a related party?
options: The loss is disallowed and can never be used.
The loss is disallowed, but the related party can use the disallowed loss to offset a gain when they subsequently sell the asset.
The loss is disallowed, but the seller can use the disallowed loss to offset future gains from unrelated parties.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Losses from sales to related parties are disallowed. However, if the related party later sells the asset at a gain, they can reduce that gain by the amount of the previously disallowed loss. This prevents tax avoidance within families or affiliated entities.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about the purpose of the related party rules: to prevent artificial losses within a family or controlled entity.

rows.label: Goal
values: Realize gains to utilize lower tax brackets or offset expiring capital losses.
Realize losses to offset capital gains and up to $3,000 of ordinary income.

rows.label: Market Conditions
values: May be beneficial in rising markets or when anticipating future tax rate increases.
Most beneficial in declining markets or when holding assets with unrealized losses.

rows.label: Tax Impact
values: Creates a current tax liability on the realized gain.
Generates a current tax benefit by reducing taxable income.

title: Alternative Minimum Tax (AMT)
description: Explain the AMT calculation process and apply AMT planning strategies.
topics: Identify common AMT preference items and adjustments

sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: The AMT is a parallel tax system designed to ensure high-income taxpayers pay at least a minimum amount of tax.

sections.title: Why AMT Exists
sections.content: Original purpose: Prevent wealthy taxpayers from using deductions and preferences to pay little or no tax. After TCJA (2017), far fewer taxpayers are subject to AMT due to higher exemptions and SALT cap.

sections.title: AMT Calculation Formula
rows: Tax benefits not allowed for AMT
6. Pay greater of TMT or regular tax
Only pay AMT if TMT > regular

sections.title: Exemption Phase-out Rate
sections.content: Exemption reduced by 25 cents per dollar over threshold.

sections.title: AMT Adjustments - Added Back to Taxable Income
SALT Is Key
sections.content: Even with $10,000 cap, adding back SALT is often the largest AMT adjustment.

sections.title: AMT Calculation Example
sections.content: Regular taxable income: $200,000 | State income tax: $12,000 | ISO exercise spread: $80,000 | AMTI = $200,000 + $12,000 + $80,000 = $292,000 | Less Exemption (MFJ): ($133,300) | AMT base: $158,700 | AMT (26% × $158,700): $41,262 | Regular tax (assume): $35,000 | AMT owed: $41,262 - $35,000 = $6,262

sections.title: AMT Credit
sections.content: AMT paid due to timing differences (like ISOs) creates a credit that can be used against regular tax in future years when regular tax exceeds TMT. Carries forward indefinitely.

sections.title: AMT Planning Strategies - Before Year-End
items: Defer ISO exercise: Spread across years
Calculate AMT exposure: Before making decisions
Bunch deductions: If near AMT threshold
Consider SALT timing: Property tax payments

sections.title: ISO Planning Strategies
items: Model scenarios: Know AMT impact before exercising
Same-year sale: Disqualifying disposition avoids AMT
Multi-year exercise: Stay below AMT threshold
Set aside cash: For AMT payment

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: AMT = Parallel system; pay greater of AMT or regular tax
AMTI = Regular taxable income + adjustments + preferences
Main triggers: ISO exercise, SALT (though capped), private activity bonds
Exemption phases out at 25% rate over threshold
AMT credit: Recovers timing-related AMT when regular tax exceeds TMT
